---------------------------------------------------------------------------
React
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

    React (developed by Facebook/Meta) is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces
    Bundlers = Combines multiple JS/CSS files into optimized bundles for deployment. e.g parcel, webpack, vite
    node_modules is the folder where npm/yarn/pnpm/bun installs all the project dependencies
    AbortController is a Web API that allows cancelling asynchronous operations like fetch by passing a signal and calling abort.
    Turbopack = A Rust-based bundler in NEXT.js

    Vite
        1. Type: Next-gen bundler + dev server
        2. Language: Built with ESBuild (Go) for dev and Rollup for prod builds.
        3. Vite doesn’t bundle everything upfront. Instead, during development, 
           it serves your files as native ES modules directly to the browser. 
           Only when you build for production does it bundle and optimize using Rollup.

    parcel
        1. Type: Zero-config bundler
        2. Language: Rust (v2 onwards)
        3. Parcel automatically detects dependencies and parallelizes builds using worker threads. You don’t need a config file — it infers everything

    Webpack
        1. Webpack is a powerful static module bundler for modern JavaScript applications
        2. capable of transforming, bundling, or packaging just about any resource or asset
        3. What Webpack Does
              Module Bundling: Combines multiple JavaScript files into one or more bundles
              Asset Processing: Handles CSS, images, fonts, and other static assets
              Code Splitting: Splits code into chunks for better performance
              Hot Module Replacement: Updates modules in the browser without full reload
              Tree Shaking: Removes unused code from the final bundle
              Optimization: Minifies, compresses, and optimizes code for production

    Babel ( Abstract Syntax Tree )
        1. Babel is a JavaScript compiler that transforms modern JavaScript (ES6+) into backward-compatible
        2. Older browsers may not support these features. Babel acts as a transpiler, converting modern JS to a version that all browsers can execute
        3. How Babel Works
            1. Parsing -> Converts code into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
            2. Transformation -> Applies transformations like polyfilling, transpiling, and removing unsupported syntax.
            3. Code Generation -> Converts the transformed AST back into JavaScript.
        4. Babel Key Components
            1. Core -> The Babel compiler itself.
            2. Presets -> Bundles of plugins for specific transformations
            3. Plugins -> Individual transformations like class properties or optional chaining.
            4. Polyfills -> Introduce missing functions or objects
        5. Advanced Features in Babel
            1. Tree Shaking (Dead Code Elimination)
            2. Babel Polyfills vs. Transpilation
                1. Polyfills: Add missing functionality for older browsers
                2. Transpilation: Converts new syntax to an older equivalent.
        6. Limitations of Babel
            1. Does not provide runtime support: Needs polyfills for missing APIs like fetch().
            2. Does not handle TypeScript type-checking: Needs tsc or ESLint for that.

    Source Maps (types : hidden-source-map, eval, source-map )
        1. A source map is a file that maps your minified or transpiled JavaScript code back to its original source code
        2. This allows browsers, debuggers, and developers to trace errors in the original files instead of unreadable, compressed code
        3. Minified Code : 
            function add(a,b){return a+b}console.log(add(5,10));

        How does a browser know where to find the source map file?
        👉 The minified JS file contains //# sourceMappingURL=file.js.map. This tells the browser where to look

    CRA vs Vite
        A. CRA (Create React App)
            1. Create React App (CRA) is the official tool developed by Meta (Facebook) to quickly set up a React project without manual configuration
            2. Uses Webpack & Babel under the hood. [Webpack  : Webpack is a module bundler that helps in optimizing, transforming, and bundling JavaScript]
            3. Supports Progressive Web App (PWA) out-of-the-box
        B. VITE
            1. Vite (French for "fast") is a modern build tool and development server that provides a blazing-fast development experience for React, Vue, Svelte, and more
            2. Uses ES Modules (ESM) instead of Webpack bundling (40x faster than Webpack)
            3. Supports SSR (Server-Side Rendering) out-of-the-box

        C. Why Vite is Faster Than CRA?
            1. No upfront bundling – Vite serves unbundled ES modules directly.
            2. On-demand Compilation – Only compiles files when requested.
            3. Uses ESBuild – 40x faster than Babel/Webpack.
            4. Faster HMR – Updates are near instantaneous.

    Real DOM
        1. The actual browser DOM that the user sees.
        1. Manipulations here affect the UI directly.
        1. React tries to avoid touching it directly too often because updates are slow

    Virtual DOM
        1. Virtual DOM (VDOM) is an in-memory representation of the real DOM elements. It’s a lightweight JavaScript object that React uses to track changes in the UI without touching the real DOM
        2. How It Works (High-Level Explanation)
            1. Render -> The UI is first created in memory as a Virtual DOM tree.
            2. Diffing -> When the UI updates, a new Virtual DOM is created and compared with the previous one.
            3. Reconciliation -> The minimal set of changes is calculated and applied to the real DOM.

    Reconciliation | React Fiber (React Fiber is the reconciliation algorithm introduced in React 16)
        1. Reconciliation is the process React uses to efficiently update the real DOM by applying only the necessary changes based on the Virtual DOM diffing algorithm
        2. Reconciliation Process - Diffing Algorithm [heuristic-based algorithm] = Runs inside the reconciliation process
            1. React re-renders the component when state or props change.
            2. A new Virtual DOM tree is created.
            3. React compares (diffs) the new Virtual DOM with the previous one.
            4. It calculates the minimal changes needed and updates only those parts in the real DOM.

    Diffing Algorithm
        1. React uses an efficient Diffing Algorithm (O(n) complexity) to compare the old and new Virtual DOM trees instead of a deep comparison (O(n³)).
        2. How the Diffing Algorithm Works
            1. Compare Element Types - Changing <div> to <p> causes a full re-render.
            2. Compare Child Elements (Key Optimization) - If keys are missing or change incorrectly, React removes and recreates items instead of updating them efficiently.

    jsx
        1. JSX stands for JavaScript XML.
        1. It’s a syntax extension for JavaScript that looks like HTML, but compiles down to plain JavaScript.
        1. It allows you to write UI in a declarative, HTML-like syntax inside JavaScript.
        2. JSX Rules / Syntax
            Single root element -> Must wrap everything in one parent (or use <> </> fragments).
            Expressions inside {} -> You can embed JS values, function calls, variables.
            Attributes use camelCase (e.g. className, onClick).
            JSX prevents injection attacks — values inside {} are automatically escaped. prevent XSS.
            Conditionals -> Ternaries or logical AND (&&), since if/else doesn’t work inside JSX directly.
            Lists -> Use .map() to render arrays, and always provide key props.
            Comments -> {/  comment here  /}.

    Hooks
        useState
            1. useState is a React Hook that lets you add state to functional components
            2. Before hooks (pre React 16.8), only class components had state. Now, functional components can be fully stateful.
            3. setState does not update immediately — React schedules it. Don’t rely on the state right after calling setState
            4. Setting state to same value still re-renders (React compares by reference, not deep equality)
            5. State updates are async & batched -> multiple setState calls in same event loop flush together.
            6. e.g const [state, setState] = useState(initialState);

        useReducer
            1. Alternative to useState for complex state logic.
            2. Accepts a reducer function [(state, action) => newState] and an initial state.
            3. Returns [state, dispatch].
            4. Best when state transitions are complex/multiple.
            5. Helps avoid deeply nested useState.
            6. e.g.const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, initialState);

        useContext
            1. Allows passing data through component tree without props drilling.
            2. Works with [React.createContext()]
            3. Returns the current context value for the given context.
            4. Must be used inside a matching [<Context.Provider>]
            5. Rerenders when context value changes.
            6. Doesn’t trigger re-render if provider’s value is referentially same.
            7. Only works inside matching provider.
            8. e.g. const value = useContext(MyContext);

        useRef
            1. Returns a mutable ref object: { current: initialValue }.
            2. Value persists across renders but doesn’t trigger re-render. 
            3. Commonly used for DOM refs (ref={myRef}) or storing mutable values.
            4. Great for caching previous values, timers, or external libraries.
            5. Different from state: updating .current does not re-render. Updating .current is synchronous
            6. e.g.
                    const inputRef = useRef(null);
                    inputRef.current.focus();

        useLayoutEffect
            1. Runs synchronously after DOM mutations but before paint (can block rendering).
            2. Good for measuring DOM nodes and layout calculations.
            3. Blocks the paint -> can cause performance issues if overused.
            4. Runs before useEffect. Same cleanup behavior as useEffect
            5. Use when you need to mutate DOM before browser paints.
            6. e.g.
                    useLayoutEffect(() => { 
                        /  measure DOM  / 
                    }, []);

        useEffect
            1. Runs after render (async, non-blocking).
            2. Used for side effects: data fetching, subscriptions, timers, logging.
            3. Cleanup function returned runs before next effect or on unmount.
            3. Dependency array controls re-run.
                [] -> run once on mount
                [x] -> run when x changes
                no array -> run after every render
            4. Beware of stale closures (capture old values).
            5. e.g.
                    useEffect(() => { 
                        fetchData(); return () => cleanup(); 
                    }, [id]);

        useImperativeHandle
            1. Customizes the value exposed via ref when using forwardRef.
            2. Lets parent control child’s methods imperatively.
            3. Should be used sparingly — React favors declarative.
            4. Typically for exposing focus/scroll/animation APIs.
            5. Works only inside forwardRef.
            6. e.g.
                    useImperativeHandle(ref, () => ({ 
                        focus: () => inputRef.current.focus() 
                    }));

        useCallback
            1. Memoizes a function between renders.
            2. Prevents unnecessary re-creation of callbacks (useful in child props).
            3. Dependencies control when function is recreated.
            4. Helps avoid child re-renders with React.memo.
            5. Avoid overuse — use only when passing functions to children or expensive closures.
            6. e.g.
                    const memoFn = useCallback(() => { 
                        ... 
                    }, [deps]);

        useMemo
            1. Memoizes a computed value between renders.
            2. Runs the function only when dependencies change.
            3. Useful for expensive calculations or derived state.
            4. Similar to useCallback but for values, not functions.
            5. Avoid premature optimization — use only for heavy computations.
            6. Similar to useCallback but for values, not functions.
            7. e.g.
                    const memoValue = useMemo(() =>{
                        computeExpensiveValue(a, b)
                    } [a, b]);

        useDeferredValue - how it differs from debounce
            1.  tells React: "this value can lag behind. Prioritize more urgent updates first.
                React's concurrent scheduler decides when to update
                If a new value comes in, React abandons the stale deferred render
                Keeping input responsive while a heavy re-render happens
            2.  In short: debounce is about delaying execution by a fixed time. useDeferredValue is about deprioritizing a render so urgent updates (typing) aren't blocked. It adapts to the user's device speed.
            3.  e.g  
                    const [query, setQuery] = useState("");
                    const deferredQuery = useDeferredValue(query);

        useTransition - for non-urgent state updates
            Marks a state update as non-urgent, allowing React to keep the UI responsive during expensive re-renders.
            code 
                    const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition();
                    function handleFilterChange(newFilter: string) {
                        startTransition(() => {
                            setFilter(newFilter); // This update is "transitional" — React can interrupt it
                        });
                    }

                    return (
                        <>
                            <FilterBar onChange={handleFilterChange} />
                            {isPending ? <Spinner /> : <ResultsList filter={filter} />}
                        </>
                    );

    Fragment
        1. A Fragment is a special React component that lets you group multiple children without adding extra nodes to the DOM
        1. React introduced Fragments in v16.2.
        1. improves performance, keeps HTML semantics correct
        1. Ways to Write -> <></>  <React.Fragment></React.Fragment>

    Pure Component / React.memo
        1. A Pure Component in React is a class component that implements shouldComponentUpdate with a shallow prop and state comparison
        1. Why - Avoids unnecessary re-renders -> improves performance.
        1. Pure components re-render only when props/state actually change
        1. For function components, you use React.memo instead of PureComponent
        1. PureComponents only do shallow comparison, so nested changes won’t be detected
        1. e.g

            class Counter extends React.PureComponent {
                render() {
                    console.log("Rendering Counter");
                    return <h1>{this.props.count}</h1>;
                }
            }

            function App() {
                const [count, setCount] = React.useState(0);
                return (
                    <>
                        <Counter count={count} />
                        <button onClick={() => setCount(count)}>Re-render</button>
                    </>
                );
            }

            What Happens Here:
            When you click the button, you call setCount(count) — but the value hasn’t changed (it’s still 0).
                React triggers a re-render in <App />, but:
                <Counter /> is a PureComponent.
                Its prop (count) didn’t actually change (still 0).
                So React skips re-rendering Counter.
                You’ll see that "Rendering Counter" doesn’t log again.

    Component Lifecycle
        Every React component (especially class components) goes through a series of phases
        
        1. Mounting -> component is created & inserted into DOM
            1. constructor()                     -> Initialize state, bind methods.
            1. static getDerivedStateFromProps() -> Rarely used; update state based on props before render.
            1. render()                          -> Returns JSX (pure function, no side effects).
            1. componentDidMount()               -> Called once after first render. Perfect for API calls, subscriptions, DOM access.

        2. Updating -> Component is re-rendered due to changes in props or state.
            1. static getDerivedStateFromProps()                  -> Runs before every render when props change.
            1. shouldComponentUpdate(nextProps, nextState)        -> Decide whether to re-render. Useful for performance.
            1. render()                                           -> Re-renders UI.
            1. getSnapshotBeforeUpdate(prevProps, prevState)      -> Capture info (like scroll position) before DOM updates.
            1. componentDidUpdate(prevProps, prevState, snapshot) -> Called after render, good for reacting to prop/state changes (e.g., fetch new data when prop changes).

        3. Unmounting -> Component is removed from the DOM.
            1. componentWillUnmount() -> Cleanup: cancel timers, remove subscriptions, abort API calls.

        4. Functional Components Equivalent
            componentDidMount → useEffect(() => {...}, [])
            componentDidUpdate → useEffect(() => {...}, [deps])
            componentWillUnmount → useEffect(() => { return cleanup }, [])
            shouldComponentUpdate → handled with React.memo / useMemo / useCallback.
            getSnapshotBeforeUpdate → useLayoutEffect

    React Portal
        1. A Portal lets you render children into a different part of the DOM tree that exists outside the parent component’s DOM hierarchy
        1. Portals don’t break React’s event bubbling → events still bubble up through the React tree, not the DOM tree.
        1. Why -   Normally, React renders a component inside its parent’s DOM node.
                But sometimes you need to “escape” that — e.g., modals, dialogs, dropdowns, tooltips — so they are not affected by parent’s CSS overflow/position/z-index
        2. e.g
                Without Portal:                     With Portal:
                ------------------------------------------------------------
                #root                               #root
                └─ App                              └─ App
                    ├─ <h1>App Content</h1>             └─ <h1>App Content</h1>
                    └─ <div class="modal">...        #modal-root
                                                        └─ <div class="modal">...

    React Fiber
        React Fiber is a complete rewrite of React’s reconciliation engine, introduced in React 16 (2017).
        It enables concurrent, interruptible, prioritized, incremental rendering.
        Before Fiber, React used a synchronous recursive stack-based reconciler which could not pause work.
        Fiber makes React capable of pausing, resuming, and aborting rendering, improving responsiveness.
        A Fiber is a JavaScript object representing a unit of work—a lightweight virtual stack frame.
        Each Fiber corresponds to:
            A React component (function/class)
            A DOM element
            A text node
            A Suspense boundary
            A context provider/consumer node
        Fiber introduced the two-phase architecture:
            Render Phase: async, pausable, builds Fiber tree
            Commit Phase: sync, applies DOM changes
        Fiber is the foundation for Hooks, Suspense, Transitions, Time Slicing, and Concurrent Rendering (React 18).

    React Router
        1. React Router is the standard routing library for React apps.
        1. It enables client-side routing → means navigation happens in the browser without full page reloads.
        1. Types
            1. Router Types
                1. BrowserRouter → Uses HTML5 history API (pushState, popState). Standard for web apps.
                1. HashRouter → Uses URL hash (/#/path). Good for static file hosting (e.g., GitHub Pages).
                1. MemoryRouter → Stores history in memory

            2. Route & Routes
                Routes → Wrapper that contains all your route definitions.
                Route → Maps a path (/about) to a component
                e.g
                    import { BrowserRouter, Routes, Route } from "react-router-dom";

                    function App() {
                        return (
                            <BrowserRouter>
                                <Routes>
                                    <Route path="/" element={<Home />} />
                                    <Route path="/about" element={<About />} />
                                </Routes>
                            </BrowserRouter>
                        );
                    }

            3. Link & NavLink
                Link → Navigate between routes without reloading page.
                NavLink → Like Link, but applies active styles when URL matches
                e.g
                    <Link to="/about">Go to About</Link>
                    <NavLink to="/about" className={({ isActive }) => isActive ? "active" : ""}>
                        About
                    </NavLink>

        1. Hooks in React Router v6
            useNavigate() → programmatic navigation.
            useParams() → access route params (e.g., /user/:id).
            useLocation() → get current URL/location object.
            useSearchParams() → read/write query strings.
            useRoutes() → define routes using objects instead of JSX.

    React StrictMode
        1. Identifies unsafe lifecycle methods
        1. Warns about deprecated APIs
        1. Detects legacy string refs
        1. Detects unexpected side-effects in constructors, render, etc
        1. Logs twice in dev (because of StrictMode) but only once in production. 
            useEffect(() => {
                console.log("Effect runs");
            }, []);
        
        1. e.g
                ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById("root")).render(
                    <React.StrictMode>
                        <App />
                    </React.StrictMode>
                );

                // Basic inline creating the React apk with cdn links in html file of ReactDOM, React, Babel
                const root = ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root'));
                root.render(<App />);

    React Lazy and Suspense
        React.lazy() lets you dynamically import a component and load it only when it’s rendered.
        It enables code splitting, reducing the initial bundle size.
        Works only with default exports.
        Lazy components cannot be rendered outside a Suspense boundary
        <Suspense> is a wrapper component that shows a fallback UI while the lazy component is loading
        e.g
            import React, { Suspense } from 'react';
            const LazyComponent = React.lazy(() => import('./HeavyComponent'));
            function App() {
                return (
                    <div>
                        <h1>Hello React</h1>
                        <Suspense fallback={<div>Loading...</div>}>
                            <LazyComponent />
                        </Suspense>
                    </div>
                );
            }
            export default App;

    package.json
        Purpose: The manifest file for your project.
        Contains:
        Project metadata (name, version, author, license, scripts).
        Dependency ranges (e.g., "react": "^18.2.0").
        Dev dependencies, peer dependencies.
        Scripts (npm run build, npm start).
        Human-written and human-edited.
        Portable — shared with everyone in the repo.
        Versioning style: (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH)
            "^1.2.3" → any compatible minor,patch (1.x.x). 
            "~1.2.3" → any patch version (1.2.x).

    package-lock.json
        Purpose: The snapshot of your dependency tree.
        Generated automatically when you run npm install.
        Ensures deterministic installs across environments.
        Not meant for manual editing.
        Contains:
            Exact resolved versions of every package (and sub-dependency).
            Integrity checksums for security.
            Structure of dependency tree.

    Differences - 
        
        1. export default & export

            | Feature        | `export default`       | `export` (named)                        |
            | -------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
            | Count per file | Only   one   allowed   | Multiple allowed                        |
            | Import name    | Can be anything        | Must match export name (unless aliased) |
            | Use case       | Main thing from a file | Utilities, helpers, multiple exports    |
            | Import syntax  | `import X from '...'`  | `import { X, Y } from '...'`            |

        2. Service worker & Web worker

            | Feature       | Service Worker                      | Web Worker                               |
            | ------------- | ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
            | Purpose       | Network proxy, offline caching      | Background computation                   |
            | Lifecycle     | Independent of page, persistent     | Tied to the page, stops when page closes |
            | Access        | Can intercept network requests      | Cannot intercept network requests        |
            | Main use case | Offline support, push notifications | Heavy computation without UI blocking    |
            | DOM access    | No                                  | No                                       |

        3. Class & functional component

            | Feature / Aspect      | Class Component                                          | Functional Component                                    |
            | --------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------| ------------------------------------------------------- |
            |   Definition          | ES6 class that extends `React.Component`                 | JavaScript function returning JSX                       |
            |   State Management    | `this.state` + `this.setState()`                         | `useState` and other Hooks                              |
            |   Lifecycle Methods   | Full set: `componentDidMount`, `shouldComponentUpdate`,  | Hooks: `useEffect`, `useLayoutEffect` replace lifecycle |
            |                       |           `componentDidMount`, `shouldComponentUpdate`,  |                                                          |
            |   `this` Binding      | Needs `this` binding for methods                         | No `this` required                                      |
            |   Syntax Complexity   | Verbose; more boilerplate                                | Concise and cleaner syntax                              |
            |   Performance         | Slightly slower historically, larger bundle              | Usually faster, especially with hooks & memoization     |
            |   Hooks Support       | Not supported                                            | Fully supported                                         |
            |   Side Effects        | Handled in lifecycle methods                             | Handled via `useEffect` and other hooks                 |
            |   Reusability         | Reusable via HOCs, render props                          | Reusable via custom hooks                               |
            |   Best Use Case       | Legacy codebases or complex lifecycle-heavy apps         | Modern apps, prefer hooks for simplicity                |

        4. controlled vs uncontrolled components

            | Feature / Aspect     | Controlled Component                                   | Uncontrolled Component                           |
            | -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------ |
            |   State Management   | React state (`useState` or `this.state`)               | DOM handles state; accessed via `ref`            |
            |   Value Binding      | `value={state}`                                        | No `value` binding; use `defaultValue` if needed |
            |   Updates            | `onChange` handler updates React state                 | DOM updates automatically                        |
            |   Form Submission    | Use React state to read values                         | Use `ref` to read values                         |
            |   Predictability     | Highly predictable, React is source of truth           | Less predictable, React is not source of truth   |
            |   Validation         | Easy to validate on every change                       | Validation usually done on submit                |
            |   Best Use Case      | Dynamic forms, real-time validation, React-driven apps | Simple forms, quick prototypes, or legacy code   |
            |   Boilerplate        | More code for handlers                                 | Less code, simpler                               |

        5.  SSR vs CSR

            | Feature / Aspect       | CSR (Client-Side Rendering)              | SSR (Server-Side Rendering)                                                         |
            | ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
            |   Rendering            | Browser renders via JS                   | Server renders full HTML                                                            |
            |   Initial Load         | Slower (needs JS to render)              | Faster (HTML ready immediately)                                                     |
            |   SEO                  | Poor for crawlers unless pre-rendered    | Better SEO; HTML content available immediately                                      |
            |   Interactivity        | Hydration needed for React interactivity | React hydrates after HTML is loaded                                                 |
            |   Performance          | Fast navigation after initial load (SPA) | Initial load faster, subsequent navigations slower unless using client-side routing |
            |   Bundle Size          | JS heavy                                 | Can split JS; smaller initial payload possible                                      |
            |   Use Case             | SPAs with dynamic content, dashboards    | Public pages, blogs, e-commerce product pages                                       |
            |   Framework Examples   | React, Vue, Angular SPA                  | Next.js, Nuxt.js, Remix                                                             |

        6. React vs Angular

            | Feature / Aspect           | React                                                                 | Angular                                                                        |
            | -------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
            |   Type                     | Library for building UI                                               | Full-fledged framework                                                         |
            |   Language                 | JavaScript / TypeScript (optional)                                    | TypeScript (mandatory)                                                         |
            |   Data Binding             | One-way binding (default), two-way via `useState` & controlled inputs | Two-way binding (`[(ngModel)]`)                                                |
            |   DOM                      | Virtual DOM                                                           | Real DOM with change detection                                                 |
            |   Learning Curve           | Moderate; simpler core concepts                                       | Steep; many built-in concepts (DI, RxJS, modules)                              |
            |   Component Architecture   | Functional + class components                                         | Component + Module + Service architecture                                      |
            |   State Management         | Hooks (`useState`, `useReducer`), external libs like Redux            | Services + RxJS, NgRx for complex state                                        |
            |   Performance              | High due to Virtual DOM                                               | Slightly slower for complex apps, but optimized with change detection strategy |
            |   Routing                  | React Router (external)                                               | Built-in Angular Router                                                        |
            |   Tooling / CLI            | Create React App, Vite, Next.js                                       | Angular CLI (strongly opinionated)                                             |
            |   Flexibility              | Very flexible; choose libraries as needed                             | Opinionated; many features built-in                                            |
            |   Community & Ecosystem    | Very large, strong ecosystem                                          | Large, enterprise-focused                                                      |
            |   Use Case                 | SPAs, dashboards, modern web apps                                     | Enterprise apps, full-featured applications                                    |

    How browser render web page

        browser-rendering/
        │
        ├─ HTML/                  # The HTML file requested from the server
        │  └─ comment: "Main markup, parsed first to start DOM tree"
        │
        ├─ DOM/                   # Document Object Model
        │  └─ comment: "Tree of HTML elements; represents structure"
        │
        ├─ CSS/                   # CSS files (inline & external)
        │  └─ CSSOM/              # CSS Object Model
        │      └─ comment: "Parsed CSS rules and styles applied to DOM nodes"
        │
        ├─ RenderTree/             # Combination of DOM + CSSOM
        │  └─ comment: "Visible elements with computed styles"
        │
        ├─ Layout/                 # Reflow step
        │  └─ comment: "Calculates size & position of each element"
        │
        ├─ Paint/                  # Rasterization step
        │  └─ comment: "Fills pixels for backgrounds, text, borders, shadows"
        │
        ├─ Compositing/            # GPU layers
        │  └─ comment: "Layers combined to produce final image efficiently"
        │
        └─ Screen/                 # Pixels displayed to user
            └─ comment: "Final rendered page seen by user"

    Web Accessibility (a11y)
        1. Web Accessibility (a11y) is the practice of building web applications that can be used by everyone, 
           including people with disabilities such as visual, auditory, motor, and cognitive impairments.
           Makes apps usable for people with screen readers, keyboard-only navigation, or color blindness
        1. “a11y” = accessibility (a + 11 letters + y)    
        1. WCAG :- (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) — a standard defining how to make web content accessible
           Principles
            1. Perceivable    :- Content must be visible/hearable Example: alt text for images
            2. Operable       :- Users must be able to interact Example: keyboard navigation
            3. Understandable :- UI should be predictable Example: clear labels, error messages
            4. Robust         :- Works across browsers and assistive tech
        1. Techniques
            1. Semantic HTML -> <h1>Main Title</h1>
            2. ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) Attributes -> <button aria-label="Close modal">X</button>
            3. Keyboard Navigation -> <div tabIndex={0}></div>
            4. Focus Management -> useEffect(() => {modalRef.current.focus();}, []);
            5. Alt Text for Images

    Web Performance Metrics | Core Web Vitals
        1. Metrics that measure how quickly a web page loads, renders, and responds to user interactions.
        1. Helps identify bottlenecks and improve user experience (UX).
        1. Often measured using Lighthouse, Chrome DevTools
        1. Key Metrics
            1. Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)
                Measures loading performance.
                When the largest visible element (image, heading, text block) is fully rendered.
                Good score: ≤ 2.5s

            2. First Input Delay (FID)
                Measures interactivity.
                Time between user’s first interaction (click, tap, keypress) and browser responding.
                Good score: ≤ 100ms

            3. Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)
                Measures visual stability.
                Calculates unexpected layout shifts during page load (ads, images without dimensions).
                Good score: ≤ 0.1

            4. First Contentful Paint (FCP)
                Time until first piece of DOM content is rendered (text, image, SVG).
                Good score: ~1s–1.8s

            5. Time to Interactive (TTI)
                Time until the page is fully interactive (all scripts loaded and event handlers ready).
                Good score: ≤ 3–5s

            6. Total Blocking Time (TBT)
                Sum of all time periods long tasks block the main thread between FCP and TTI.
                Reduces responsiveness; smaller TBT → better FID

    How to improve SEO of Website
        Semantic HTML + meta tags
        Fast performance (Core Web Vitals)
        SSR (Next.js) for React
        Mobile-friendly + accessible
        Clean URLs + sitemap

    ngrok
        1. ngrok is a cross-platform tool that creates secure tunnels from your local machine to the internet.
        2. It allows you to expose a local web server (running on localhost) to the outside world via a public URL
        3. It’s a tunneling / reverse proxy service.
        4. Supports HTTP, HTTPS, TCP, TLS tunnels
        5. One Liner - ngrok is a tunneling service that exposes local applications to the internet using secure public URLs, commonly used for webhook testing, client demos, and quick development sharing.

    JavaScript package managers
        1. JavaScript package managers used to install, manage, version, and run dependencies in JS projects. e.g NPM, PNPM, YARN

        | Feature                  |  npm                |  yarn              |  pnpm                    |
        | ------------------------ | ------------------- | ------------------ | -------------------------|
        | Maintained by            | npm, Inc.           | Meta (Facebook)    | Open-source community    |
        | Comes with Node.js       |   Yes               |   No               |   No                     |
        | Default package manager  |   Yes               |   No               |   No                     |
        | Install speed            | Medium              | Fast               |  Fastest                 |
        | Disk usage               |   High              |   High             |    Very Low              |
        | Dependency storage       | Copies per project  | Copies per project |  Global store + symlinks |
        | `node_modules` structure | Flat / nested       | Flat               |  Strict, symlinked       |
        | Phantom dependencies     |   Possible          |   Possible         |    Prevented             |
        | Lock file                | `package-lock.json` | `yarn.lock`        | `pnpm-lock.yaml`         |
        | Deterministic installs   |   Yes               |   Yes              |    Yes (strict)          |
        | Monorepo support         | Basic (workspaces)  | Strong             |  Best-in-class           |
        | Workspace support        |   Yes               |   Yes              |    Yes                   |
        | Peer dependency handling | Loose               | Medium             |  Strict                  |
        | CI performance           | Medium              | Fast               |  Fastest                 |
        | Caching                  | Basic               | Advanced           |  Highly optimized        |
        | Offline install          |   Limited           |   Yes              |   Yes                    |
        | Learning curve           | Low                 | Medium             | Medium                   |

        
        | Task               | npm                   | yarn                  | pnpm                |
        | ------------------ | --------------------- | --------------------- | ------------------- |
        | Install deps       | `npm install`         | `yarn`                | `pnpm install`      |
        | Add dependency     | `npm i react`         | `yarn add react`      | `pnpm add react`    |
        | Add dev dependency | `npm i -D vite`       | `yarn add -D vite`    | `pnpm add -D vite`  |
        | Remove dependency  | `npm uninstall react` | `yarn remove react`   | `pnpm remove react` |
        | Run script         | `npm run dev`         | `yarn dev`            | `pnpm dev`          |
        | Global install     | `npm i -g pkg`        | `yarn global add pkg` | `pnpm add -g pkg`   |

    GraphQL
        GraphQL is a query language for APIs and a runtime that allows clients to request exactly the data they need
        We can use the GraphQL using Apollo client
        Is GraphQL a database? - No. It’s a query language for APIs, not storage
        
        Why Not REST
            Over-fetching
            Under-fetching
            Multiple endpoints
            API versioning headaches.
        
        Core GraphQL Building Blocks
            Schema (The Contract) - The schema defines what data is available, Single source of truth, Strongly typed
            Queries (Read data)
            Mutations (Write data)
            Subscriptions (Real-time) - Uses WebSockets, Live updates (chat, notifications)
            Resolvers (Where Logic Lives) - Resolvers map schema → actual data.
        
        Execution Flow
            Client sends query
            Schema validates query
            Resolvers execute
            Response matches query shape
            Errors returned with partial data
        
        Caching
            GraphQL does not cache by default.
            Caching options:
                Client caching (Apollo, Relay)
                CDN with persisted queries
                Field-level caching
                Response normalization
        
        Advantages
            Precise data fetching
            Fewer network requests
            Strong typing
            Excellent developer experience
            Ideal for frontend-heavy apps
            Great for mobile & slow networks
        
        Disadvantages
            Complex server setup
            Harder caching at HTTP level
            Query cost abuse (DoS risk)
            Overkill for simple CRUD
            Requires strict schema discipline

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Redux + Zustand
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

    A predictable, centralized state container for JavaScript apps where state is immutable and updated via actions and reducers
    It manages the global state in a single place and makes state changes predictable.
    Works well with React but can be used with other frameworks too.
    * In Redux ToolKit Slice = Action + Reducer
    * Everything is traceable — every state change has an action, every action is logged in DevTools with a before/after diff. This is Redux's biggest strength for large apps.

    Core Concepts
        1. Store -> Central object that holds the entire application state. Only one store per app is recommended.
            e.g 
                import { createStore } from 'redux';
                const store = createStore(reducer);

        2. Action -> Plain JavaScript object describing what happened. Must have a type field.
            e.g { type: 'INCREMENT' }

        3. Reducer -> Pure function that takes previous state and action, returns new state.
            e.g
                const counterReducer = (state = 0, action) => {
                    switch(action.type){
                        case 'INCREMENT': return state + 1;
                        case 'DECREMENT': return state - 1;
                        default: return state;
                    }   
                }


        4. 4. Dispatch -> Method to send an action to the store. / the only way to trigger a state change 
            e.g store.dispatch({ type: 'INCREMENT' });

        5. Selectors -> Functions to extract specific state from the store.
            e.g const count = store.getState().counter;

    Difference
        1. Redux Thunk & Redux saga

            | Feature / Aspect   | Redux Thunk                            | Redux Saga                                    |
            | ------------------ | -------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
            |   Type             | Middleware that allows async functions | Middleware using generator functions          |
            |   Complexity       | Easy, simple setup                     | More complex, steep learning curve            |
            |   Use Case         | Simple async tasks (API calls)         | Complex async flows (retry, cancel, debounce) |
            |   Code Style       | Async/await or promises                | Generator functions (`yield`)                 |
            |   Boilerplate      | Less                                   | More                                          |
            |   Performance      | Fine for small/medium apps             | Better for large-scale apps with heavy async  |
            |   Learning Curve   | Low                                    | High                                          |
            |   Community        | More common in small/medium apps       | Preferred in enterprise-level apps            |

        2. Redux & Zustand

            | Aspect             | Redux Toolkit                                                              | Zustand                                                |
            | -------------------| -------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
            | Setup              | `configureStore` + `<Provider>` wrapper + typed hooks setup                | Single `create()` call, no Provider needed             |
            | Boilerplate        | Structured: slices, reducers, actions (auto-generated but organized)       | Minimal: state + actions in one function               |
            | Update Pattern     | `dispatch(actionCreator(payload))`                                         | Call functions directly: `store.addTodo()`             |
            | Async Handling     | `createAsyncThunk` (pending/fulfilled/rejected) or middleware (thunk/saga) | Just use `async/await` inside actions                  |
            | DevTools           | Built-in, excellent time-travel debugging                                  | Via middleware, good but lighter                       |
            | Persistence        | Requires external library (redux-persist)                                  | Built-in `persist` middleware                          |
            | Immer Support      | Built-in automatically                                                     | Optional via middleware                                |
            | Bundle Size        | ~11KB (RTK) + ~2KB (react-redux)                                           | ~1.1KB                                                 |
            | Store Structure    | Single global store (recommended pattern)                                  | Multiple independent stores encouraged                 |
            | TypeScript         | Good, but requires typed hooks setup                                       | Excellent inference out of the box                     |
            | Next.js App Router | Requires `"use client"` Provider at root                                   | Only components using the hook need `"use client"`     |
            | Best For           | Large teams, strict architecture, complex async flows                      | Most apps, fast development, simplicity-first projects |

    Zustand
        Zustand is a minimal, unopinionated state management library. No providers, no boilerplate, no reducers. You create a store with a single function and use it directly as a hook.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Next JS
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    
    Next.js is a React framework that provides hybrid rendering (SSR, SSG, ISR, CSR), file-based routing, API routes, and
    built-in optimizations like image handling. Compared to CRA, it’s SEO-friendly, faster in production, and
    more feature-rich, making it ideal for modern web apps

    The Next.js Compiler, written in Rust 
    Compilation using the Next.js Compiler is 17x faster than Babel and enabled by default since Next.js version 12.

    <Image /> component → automatic resizing, lazy loading, WebP.
    Link prefetching (<Link />) for fast navigation.
    You can create backend endpoints inside pages/api/ . Eliminates need for a separate backend in small apps
    How to setup Nextjs app => npx create-next-app@latest

    Routing
        In Next.js, routes are created from the file/folder structure instead of manually configuring a router
        Every file inside the pages/ or app/ (App Router, Next 13+) folder automatically becomes a route
        
        1. Pages router
            pages/
            ┣ index.js     → /
            ┣ about.js     → /about
            ┗ blog/
                ┣ index.js  → /blog
                ┗ [id].js   → /blog/:id

        2. App router
            built on React Server Components (RSC)
            app/
            ┣ page.js           → /
            ┣ about/
            ┃   ┗ page.js       → /about
            ┗ blog/
                ┣ page.js       → /blog
                ┗ [id]/
                    ┗ page.js   → /blog/:id

    Dynamic Routing
        Dynamic routing allows you to create pages whose paths depend on dynamic data (like user IDs, slugs, product names). 
        Instead of hardcoding /products/1, /products/2

        1. Dynamic Segment
            1. Matches a single param
            1. File/Folder: pages/products/[id].js
            1. URL: /products/1 or /products/abc
            1. e.g
                    const router = useRouter();
                    const { id } = router.query;


        2. Catch-All Segment
            1. Matches multiple segments.
            1. File/Folder: pages/docs/[...slug].js
            1. URL: /docs/nextjs/routing → slug = ["nextjs", "routing"]
            1. e.g
                    const router = useRouter();
                    const { slug } = router.query;
                    return <h1>Docs Path: {slug?.join(" / ")}</h1>;
        
        3. Optional Catch-All Segment
            1. Makes the slug optional.
            1. File/Folder: pages/blog/[[...slug]].js
            1. URL: /docs → slug = undefined
            1. URL: /docs/nextjs/routing → slug = ["nextjs", "routing"]
            1. e.g 
                    const { slug } = useRouter().query;
                    if (!slug) return <h1>Blog Home</h1>;
                    return <h1>Blog Path: {slug.join(" / ")}</h1>;

    Rendering Modes
        CSR (Client-Side Rendering) → Standard React behavior.
        SSR (Server-Side Rendering) → HTML generated per request on the server.
        SSG (Static Site Generation) → HTML pre-rendered at build time.
        ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) → Static pages updated at runtime without full rebuild.
        
        (SSR) getServerSideProps → Runs on each request, great for dashboards or real-time data.
        (SSG) getStaticPaths → Used with dynamic routes to pre-generate paths at build time.
        (ISR) getStaticProps → Fetch data at build time, great for static pages.

        Difference between fallback: false, true, and blocking in getStaticPaths.
        Answer:
            false: Only paths returned at build time exist. Others → 404.
            true: Non-prebuilt paths render a fallback first, then load data client-side.
            blocking: User waits until page is fully generated server-side (no fallback UI).

    Rendering Methods
        1. CSR (Client Side Rendering) Default React way
            1. In CSR, the browser downloads a mostly empty HTML file (just a <div id="root"></div> and some script tags).
            2. Then, the React JavaScript bundle runs on the client, builds the DOM, and renders the UI.
            3. All routing, rendering, and state updates happen inside the browser after the JS loads
            4. When to use CSR
                User-specific dashboards (requires auth cookie/token).
                Pages that don’t need SEO (e.g., admin tools).
                Highly dynamic pages where pre-rendering doesn’t make sense.

        2. SSR (Server Side Rendering)
            1. In SSR, the HTML for a page is generated on the server for each request.
            2. The browser gets a fully rendered HTML document immediately (better for SEO & faster “first paint”), then React hydrates it for interactivity.
            3. In Next.js, you implement SSR with getServerSideProps.
            4. getServerSideProps runs only on the server → It never runs in the browser.
            5. You can’t use browser-only APIs (like window) inside getServerSideProps
            6. Flow of SSR
                User requests /products/1.
                Next.js calls getServerSideProps on the server.
                Data is fetched (e.g., from DB or API).
                Server generates HTML with that data and sends it to the client

                e.g 
                    export async function getServerSideProps({ params }) {
                        const res = await fetch(`https://fakestoreapi.com/products/${params.id}`);
                        const product = await res.json();
                        return { props: { initialProduct: product } };
                    }

        3. SSG (Static Site Generation) - Think of it like: "Bake the HTML once, serve it many times.
            1. Next.js generates the HTML at build time, not at request time (SSR) or in the browser (CSR).
            2. Pages are served as static files (super fast + CDN cacheable).
            3. After HTML is delivered, React hydrates the page for interactivity
            4. You must use getStaticPaths, not getServerSideProps.
            5. getStaticPaths is mandatory for dynamic routes with SSG.
            6. Flow of SSG
                You run <next build>
                Next.js executes getStaticPaths (and optionally getStaticProps).
                HTML + JSON are generated and stored as static files.
                When a user visits the page, CDN serves the prebuilt file instantly

            getStaticPaths → Tells Next.js which dynamic routes to generate at build time   .
            getStaticProps → Tells Next.js how to fetch data at build time for a single page.

        4. ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) - SSG with auto-refresh.
            1. It allows you to update static pages after the site has been built, without doing a full rebuild/redeploy.
            2. Pages are pre-rendered at build time like SSG, but Next.js can re-generate them in the background when new requests come in
            3. How ISR Works
                At build time → page is generated with getStaticPaths.
                When a user visits the page, Next.js serves the cached static HTML.
                After a set interval (revalidate), the next request will trigger a regeneration in the background.
            4. e.g
                    export async function getStaticProps({ params }) {
                        const res = await fetch(`https://fakestoreapi.com/products/${params.id}`);
                        const product = await res.json();

                        return {
                            props: { product },
                            revalidate: 60, // <-- ISR: re-generate page every 60 seconds
                        };
                    }

        5. SWR (stale-while-revalidate)
            1. SWR is a React data fetching hook (useSWR) created by Vercel
            2. It implements the HTTP stale-while-revalidate caching strategy:
                Show cached data instantly (stale).
                Re-fetch fresh data in the background (revalidate).
                Update UI automatically when fresh data arrives

    Server Components vs Client Components
        What can't you do in a Server Component?
            Server Components run only on the server and send zero JavaScript to the client. Therefore you cannot:
            useState — no state management (no interactivity)
            useEffect / useLayoutEffect — no lifecycle effects (no DOM)
            Event handlers — no onClick, onChange, onSubmit, etc.
            Browser APIs — no window, document, localStorage, navigator, IntersectionObserver, etc.
            Custom hooks that depend on state or effects
            React Context (consuming context via useContext) — though you can create a provider and pass it in a Client Component
        What you CAN do 
            async/await, direct database queries, file system access, server-only secrets, zero-bundle-cost imports.

    Can a Server Component render a Client Component?
        Yes. This is the standard pattern. A Server Component imports and renders a Client Component
        code
            // ServerComponent.tsx (Server Component)
            import ClientButton from "./ClientButton"; // has "use client"

            export default async function ServerComponent() {
            const data = await fetchData();
                return (
                    <div>
                        <h1>{data.title}</h1>
                        <ClientButton /> {/* Perfectly fine */}
                    </div>
                );
            }  

    Can a Client Component render a Server Component
        Yes, but only via composition (children prop or any React node prop). You cannot import a Server Component directly into a Client Component — that would force it to become a Client Component.
        code
            // ClientWrapper.tsx
            "use client";
            export default function ClientWrapper({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
                const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);
                return <div>{open && children}</div>; // children can be a Server Component
            }

            // page.tsx (Server Component)
            import ClientWrapper from "./ClientWrapper";
            import ServerContent from "./ServerContent"; // Server Component

            export default function Page() {
                return (
                    <ClientWrapper>
                        <ServerContent /> {/* Rendered on server, passed as children */}
                    </ClientWrapper>
                );
            }

    What is "use client" directive actually doing?
        1. use client" is a module boundary marker. It tells the bundler: "this module (and everything it imports) is the entry point into the client component graph.
        2. What it actually does
            Marks a serialization boundary — everything above this boundary runs on the server, everything at and below runs on the client.
            The bundler creates a client bundle chunk starting from this file.
            When a Server Component references this module, it doesn't inline the code — it emits a reference (a placeholder) that the client runtime resolves and hydrates.
            It does not mean the component is "client-side rendered only" — Client Components are still pre-rendered on the server (SSR'd to HTML) and then hydrated on the client with interactivity.

    searchParams
        Why prefer searchParams in Server Components?
            Better for SEO
            Server-side data fetching
            No client bundle increase
            No hydration mismatch
            Cleaner separation of concerns

        | Server Component            | Client Component         |
        | --------------------------- | ------------------------ |
        | Gets `searchParams` as prop | Uses `useSearchParams()` |
        | Runs on server              | Runs in browser          |
        | Triggers server re-render   | Updates reactively       |
        | Good for data fetching      | Good for UI state        |


    Differences
        Next.js - Create React App

            | Feature              | CRA (Create React App) | Next.js                                     |
            | -------------------- | ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
            |   Rendering          | Only CSR               | SSR, SSG, ISR, CSR                          |
            |   Routing            | Needs react-router     | Built-in file system routing                |
            |   SEO                | Poor (CSR only)        | Great (SSR/SSG/ISR)                         |
            |   Performance        | Basic                  | Image optimization, code splitting, caching |
            |   API Support        | No                     | Yes (API routes)                            |
            |   Production Ready   | Needs setup            | Out-of-the-box                              |
            |   Deployment         | Manual                 | Vercel 1-click, others supported            |

        App Router - Pages Router

            | Feature                            | Pages Router (`pages/`)                                  | App Router (`app/`)                                                  |
            | ---------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
            |   Route Definition                 | Each `.js`/`.tsx` file = route                           | Each folder with `page.js` = route                                   |
            |   Dynamic Routes                   | `[id].js` → `/blog/123`                                  | `[id]/page.js` → `/blog/123`                                         |
            |   Data Fetching                    | `getStaticProps`, `getServerSideProps`, `getStaticPaths` | Async `fetch()` in Server Components (no `get Props`)                |because it is by default server compo
            |   Layouts                          | `_app.js`, `_document.js` (global only)                  | `layout.js` per folder → supports nested layouts                     |
            |   Default Component Type           | Client Components (React as usual)                       | Server Components by default (`"use client"` needed for hooks/state) |
            |   Navigation                       | `<Link>` (needs `<a>` in old versions, fixed later)      | `<Link>` simplified, built for streaming                             |
            |   Streaming / Suspense             | Not supported                                            | Built-in support (React Suspense + streaming)                        |
            |   Parallel Routes & Interception   |  Not available                                           |  Supported (advanced routing patterns)                               |
            |   Stability                        | Mature, stable                                           | Newer (still evolving, Next.js 13+)                                  |

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Build the React apk using 2 files
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

    index.html
        <!DOCTYPE html>
        <html lang="en">
        <head>
            <meta charset="UTF-8" />
            <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0" />
            <title>Minimal React App</title>

            <!-- React and ReactDOM from CDN -->
            <script src="https://unpkg.com/react@18/umd/react.development.js" crossorigin></script>
            <script src="https://unpkg.com/react-dom@18/umd/react-dom.development.js" crossorigin></script>

            <!-- Babel for JSX support -->
            <script src="https://unpkg.com/@babel/standalone/babel.min.js"></script>
        </head>
        <body>
            <div id="root"></div>

            <!-- Your JS file -->
            <script type="text/babel" src="app.js"></script>
        </body>
        </html>

    app.js
        function App() {
        const [count, setCount] = React.useState(0);

            return (
                <div style={{ fontFamily: 'sans-serif', textAlign: 'center', marginTop: '50px' }}>
                    <h1>Hello, React without a build system!</h1>
                    <p>Count: {count}</p>
                    <button onClick={() => setCount(count + 1)}>Increment</button>
                </div>
            );
        }

        // React 18 style render
        const root = ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root'));
        root.render(<App />);

        // React 16 & 17 style render // Does not support concurrent rendering.
        ReactDOM.render(<App />, document.getElementById('root'));

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

What to Talk in Frontend Rounds
    1. how I decide SSR vs CSR
    2. my performance budget checklist
    3. my feature flag rollout
    4. Optimizations of code and components, Lazy Loading
    5. Role Management
    6. URl whitelsting and Special Character whitelisting for security
    7. Internationalization for Websites for More user base over world 
    8. Assests managements
    9. Unit / Dev Testing
    10. Mentoring and collaborate with team for prod Bugs, Doubts and Optimization of codes 
    11. Version and RollOut decisions for Releases. Branching name strategies for Feature, Canary, Rollback Changes

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

What is the Current LTS version of 
    Node 
    Npm 
    React 
    ECMA 
    NextJs

What are the features in the New versions.
How will you migrate the 
    React -> Next 
    Pages Router -> App Router

How to track Performance  :
    Chrome DevTools
    Lighthouse
    Web Vitals
    bundle analyzers
    image optimization

How to Monitoring/Analytics  :
    Sentry
    LogRocket
    Datadog RUM
    OpenTelemetry (basic)
    GA4

How to make SEO/Metadata  :
    sitemaps
    robots.txt
    Open Graph/Twitter cards
    JSON-LD structured data

What are the base liabrary to make the app faster
    UI Libraries
    Forms/Validation
    State/Data
    Internationalization
